stage.name: PC generation to fetch: where the next instruction comes from
stage.description: A focused frontend lesson on next-PC selection, prediction structures, redirects, fetch realignment, and instruction-queue handoff.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: Where does the next instruction come from?

content.title: PC generation to fetch: where the next instruction comes from
content.subtitle: The FRONTEND is the first place where control-flow guesses, redirects, cache requests, and decode pressure meet.
content.thesis: Frontend quality is not just speed. It is choosing the next PC, fetching enough bytes, and handing clean instruction work to decode.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

content.speakerNotes: This lesson reads the first two stages together because the design document treats FRONTEND as PC generation plus fetch. PC generation decides where to go next. Fetch turns that address stream into instruction entries for decode. If you understand this handoff, branch prediction, redirects, and fetch bandwidth stop feeling like disconnected topics.

actions.text: This lesson reads the first two stages together because the design document treats FRONTEND as PC generation plus fetch. PC generation decides where to go next. Fetch turns that address stream into instruction entries for decode. If you understand this handoff, branch prediction, redirects, and fetch bandwidth stop feeling like disconnected topics.

content.title: PC Gen chooses; Fetch delivers
content.subtitle: The same top-level module hosts two very different responsibilities.

callouts.title: PC Gen
callouts.body: Selects the next PC using prediction, sequential flow, and redirect feedback.

callouts.title: Fetch
callouts.body: Requests instruction data, realigns it, and fills the instruction queue.

callouts.title: Decode boundary
callouts.body: The frontend can fetch more than decode consumes in one cycle, so the queue matters.

content.takeaway: Treat FRONTEND as a controlled producer, not just an address generator.

assets.alt: Official CVA6 architecture overview block diagram
assets.caption: Official CVA6 architecture overview diagram from the CV32A60X design documentation.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

content.speakerNotes: The important split is this: PC generation chooses, fetch delivers. PC generation is about the next address. Fetch is about turning cache data into instruction work. The instruction queue matters because the frontend can fetch up to two instructions per cycle, while decode handles up to one instruction per cycle on this path. That queue is a small but important traffic buffer.

actions.text: The important split is this: PC generation chooses, fetch delivers. PC generation is about the next address. Fetch is about turning cache data into instruction work. The instruction queue matters because the frontend can fetch up to two instructions per cycle, while decode handles up to one instruction per cycle on this path. That queue is a small but important traffic buffer.

content.title: Sequential flow is only one candidate
content.subtitle: The PC generation stage combines default progress, prediction, and later-stage correction.

steps.title: Default next block
steps.body: Continue to the next 32-bit block when no stronger control decision is active.

steps.title: Predict branch or jump
steps.body: Use BTB, BHT, and RAS style structures when the decoded pattern supports prediction.

steps.title: Receive execute correction
steps.body: A misprediction discovered in execute can redirect the frontend.

steps.title: Handle commit redirects
steps.body: Commit-stage events such as flush-sensitive CSR behavior can also provide a new PC.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The PC generation stage selects the next PC using sequential flow, prediction structures such as BTB/BHT/RAS, execute-stage misprediction feedback, and commit-stage redirect cases.

content.speakerNotes: Do not think of PC generation as incrementing a counter. The default path is the next block, yes, but prediction can override that path, execute can correct a wrong guess, and commit can redirect after certain flush-sensitive cases. A frontend is a negotiation between what we think will happen and what later stages prove actually happened.

actions.text: Do not think of PC generation as incrementing a counter. The default path is the next block, yes, but prediction can override that path, execute can correct a wrong guess, and commit can redirect after certain flush-sensitive cases. A frontend is a negotiation between what we think will happen and what later stages prove actually happened.

scenes.title: Prediction structures each answer a different question

content.title: BTB, BHT, and RAS are not the same tool
content.subtitle: A professional explanation names what each structure is trying to predict.

cards.title: BTB
cards.body: Helps provide a target for branch or jump-like control-flow decisions.

cards.title: BHT
cards.body: Helps decide whether a conditional branch should be treated as taken.

cards.title: RAS
cards.body: Helps predict return addresses for return-like control flow.

cards.title: Mispredict feedback
cards.body: Later-stage evidence updates the frontend when the guess was wrong.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The PC generation stage selects the next PC using sequential flow, prediction structures such as BTB/BHT/RAS, execute-stage misprediction feedback, and commit-stage redirect cases.

content.speakerNotes: Prediction vocabulary can become alphabet soup, so keep each structure tied to a question. The branch target buffer helps with targets. The branch history table helps with taken decisions. The return address stack helps with returns. And misprediction feedback is the mechanism that prevents a guess from becoming permanent fiction.

actions.text: Prediction vocabulary can become alphabet soup, so keep each structure tied to a question. The branch target buffer helps with targets. The branch history table helps with taken decisions. The return address stack helps with returns. And misprediction feedback is the mechanism that prevents a guess from becoming permanent fiction.

scenes.title: Fetch turns address flow into decode-ready entries

content.title: Address choice is not the same as instruction delivery
content.subtitle: Fetch bridges cache-facing data and decode-facing instruction entries.

columns.title: Cache-facing side
columns.body: Fetch requests instruction data from the cache side and receives blocks that may contain multiple instruction pieces.

columns.title: Decode-facing side
columns.body: Fetch realigns instructions, places them in the instruction queue, and transmits usable work to decode.

content.takeaway: The frontend is both a control-flow machine and a formatting machine.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

content.speakerNotes: Fetch is easy to underestimate. It does not merely ask the cache for bytes and walk away. It has to realign instruction data, deal with compressed and normal instruction boundaries, place entries into the instruction queue, and then feed decode. This is why frontend lessons need both control-flow thinking and data-format thinking.

actions.text: Fetch is easy to underestimate. It does not merely ask the cache for bytes and walk away. It has to realign instruction data, deal with compressed and normal instruction boundaries, place entries into the instruction queue, and then feed decode. This is why frontend lessons need both control-flow thinking and data-format thinking.

content.title: Four questions for any frontend paragraph
content.subtitle: Use these questions before turning a frontend detail into a public claim.

items.title: Who chose the PC?
items.body: Sequential logic, prediction, execute feedback, or commit redirect?

items.title: What was fetched?
items.body: Instruction data blocks must be realigned into decode-ready instruction entries.

items.title: Where is buffering?
items.body: Instruction queue behavior explains why frontend and decode bandwidth can differ.

items.title: Who corrects mistakes?
items.body: Prediction is allowed because later stages can redirect and flush when needed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The PC generation stage selects the next PC using sequential flow, prediction structures such as BTB/BHT/RAS, execute-stage misprediction feedback, and commit-stage redirect cases.

content.speakerNotes: Here is the frontend checklist. Who chose the PC? What exactly was fetched? Where is buffering? And who corrects mistakes? These questions keep you from writing the usual vague sentence: the frontend fetches instructions. True, but not useful enough. A professional sentence names choice, delivery, buffering, and correction.

actions.text: Here is the frontend checklist. Who chose the PC? What exactly was fetched? Where is buffering? And who corrects mistakes? These questions keep you from writing the usual vague sentence: the frontend fetches instructions. True, but not useful enough. A professional sentence names choice, delivery, buffering, and correction.

questions.question: Which statement best describes FRONTEND in this lesson?

options.label: It implements PC generation and fetch responsibilities
It only writes the register file
It is unrelated to branch prediction

questions.analysis: The design document places PC generation and fetch inside FRONTEND.

questions.question: Which inputs can affect the next PC story?

options.label: Prediction structures such as BTB, BHT, or RAS

questions.analysis: The next-PC path combines normal progress, prediction, and later-stage redirects.

questions.question: Why does the instruction queue matter?

options.label: Fetch bandwidth and decode consumption can differ, so work needs a controlled handoff
It is a decorative debug feature
It proves decode handles unlimited instructions per cycle

questions.analysis: The queue buffers frontend-produced instruction entries before decode consumes them.

questions.question: What is a weak frontend explanation?

options.label: Fetch realigns instruction data before decode
Mispredict feedback can redirect the frontend

questions.analysis: The weak sentence is technically broad but misses the key responsibilities.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: PC Generation stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The PC generation stage selects the next PC using sequential flow, prediction structures such as BTB/BHT/RAS, execute-stage misprediction feedback, and commit-stage redirect cases.

content.speakerNotes: These questions are about responsibility boundaries. FRONTEND is not one vague thing. It chooses candidate addresses, predicts or redirects control flow, fetches instruction data, realigns it, buffers it, and hands it to decode. Once that is clear, the rest of the pipeline can be read with much better footing.

actions.text: These questions are about responsibility boundaries. FRONTEND is not one vague thing. It chooses candidate addresses, predicts or redirects control flow, fetches instruction data, realigns it, buffers it, and hands it to decode. Once that is clear, the rest of the pipeline can be read with much better footing.

content.title: You can now explain how instructions enter the spine
content.subtitle: PC generation chooses a path; fetch turns that path into decode-ready work.
takeaways: FRONTEND combines PC generation and fetch stages.
Prediction structures answer different next-PC questions.
Fetch realignment and the instruction queue are the bridge into decode.
content.nextStep: Next: Decode, where fetched instruction bits become backend evidence.
content.closing: Frontend work is where speculation becomes a queue of things decode can understand.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: FRONTEND module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The FRONTEND module implements PC generation and fetch, requests data from caches, realigns instructions, fills the instruction queue, and transmits instructions to DECODE.

content.speakerNotes: You can now explain the entry point of the spine. PC generation chooses a candidate path. Fetch makes that path useful by requesting data, realigning instructions, and filling a queue. Next, we cross into decode. The key question changes from where is the instruction to what does this instruction mean?
